Maternal Effect Mutations
Isolated as female sterile mutations
Are the earliest developmental mutants
Code for mRNAs provided by mother
Molecules are deposited into eggs by nurse cells

Wild-type     bicoid
bicoid lacks head and thoracic structures.
The head and thorax are replaced by duplicated posterior terminal structures of reversed polarity. Frohnhöfer H G,
Nüsslein-Volhard C
(1986) Nature (London) 324:120–125.

bcd mRNA Localization
Anterior                                                               Posterior
bicoid 3’ untranslated portion of mRNA is tethered to – end of microtubules
bcd Protein Localization
More bicoid, more anterior features

Mutant Rescue
Mutant      Mutant + cytoplasm    Mutant + bcd mRNA

Morphogens
Substances that define different cell fates in a concentration-dependent manner
Bicoid was the first morphogen described at the molecular level

Bicoid protein
Is a transcription factor
Initiates transcription of hunchback
Is a translational inhibitor
Inhibits translation of caudal
Is the primary anterior morphogen

Early
Later

Figure 4. Rescue of the nos Mutant Phenotype by In Vitro Synthesized nos Transcript(A-C) Dark-field photographs of cuticle preparations of first instar larvae derived from females homozygous for nos L7. (A) Uninjected embryo, anterior up, dorsal aspect. Note lack of abdominal segments. Head and thorasic segments are normal. (B) Partially rescued embryo, anterior up, ventral aspect. This embryo shows bilaterally asymmetric rescue and has developed six partial abdominal segments. (C) Fully rescued embryo, anterior up, ventral aspect. This pattern is indistinguishable from wild type.

nos mRNA Localization
Anterior                                                               Posterior
nanos 3’ untranslated portion of mRNA is tethered to + end of microtubules

nos Protein Localization
Anterior                                                               Posterior

Nanos
The nos protein is a translational repressor.  Its major target is the maternally supplied transcript of the hunchback gene.
Dorsal/Ventral polarity
spaetzle – follicle cells secrete into ventral side of oocyte, binds to Toll
Toll like receptors

Drosophila Mutations
Affecting Early Development
Maternal effect
Zygotic/Segmentation
Homeotic

Zygotic/Segmentation Genes
Genes that are first transcribed in the Zygote
Refine the anterior/posterior patterning into segments
Subdivided into Gap, Pair Rule, and Segment Polarity
Phenotypes of Selected Gap Mutations

Gap genes
Show a gap in segmentation
Are the first zygotic segmentation genes transcribed.
Transcription Factor
Transcription Factor

Pair rule
Cause deletions in pattern elements from every alternate segment
After gap genes have divided the body axis into rough generalized regions, pair rule gene generate more sharply defined sections.
Example of Pair rule gene expression

Segment Polarity
Mutations in segment polarity genes cause deletion of part of each segment and its replacement by a mirror image of a different part of the next segment.
Are usually expressed in stripes that are repeated with a single segment periodicity
Engrailed Expression
Summary of Segmentation Phenotypes

Drosophila Mutations
Affecting Early Development
Maternal effect
Zygotic/Segmentation
Homeotic

Homeotic Genes
Help assign a unique identity to each segment
Are regulated by the gap, pair-rule, and segment polarity genes

Wild-type
Bithorax

Wild-type
Antennapedia

Homeobox
A 60 amino acid (180 bp) region encoding a DNA binding motif.
Binds 8 bp region upstream of genes they control
Helix-turn helix motif
More than 20 Drosophila genes have homeoboxes
Highly conserved in evolution
Homeotic selector genes are encoded by two giant complexes of DNA
Bithorax Complex
